message:
feat(search): add operator-aware quote matching

Add RankedQuote interface and functions to rank quotes by both
semantic similarity and operator relevance:
- OPERATOR_QUOTE_KEYWORDS for all four operators
- computeOperatorRelevance() for keyword-based scoring
- findRelevantQuotes() combining semantic + operator scores
- getOperatorQuotes() for operator-only matching

17 new tests covering all new functionality.

Closes brenner_bot-v2zy (Operator-aware Quote Matching)
Part of brenner_bot-ukd1 (Semantic Search EPIC)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.1","title":"Feature: Hero Section & Visual Identity System","description":"# Hero Section & Visual Identity

## Purpose
The hero section is the first impression - it must instantly communicate what Brenner Lab Mode is and why it matters. Within 5 seconds, visitors should understand: "This is a serious research orchestration system that makes AI-assisted science rigorous."

## Visual Identity Goals

### Color System
- Primary: Deep science blue (#0F172A) with electric accent (#3B82F6)
- Secondary: Warm amber for Coach Mode elements (#F59E0B)
- Success green for validated hypotheses (#10B981)
- Error red for killed hypotheses (#EF4444)
- Gradients: Subtle blue-to-purple for depth

### Typography
- Headlines: Bold, confident (Inter or system font)
- Body: Readable, generous line-height
- Code: Monospace with proper syntax highlighting
- Hierarchy: Clear size progression (48px → 32px → 24px → 16px)

### Illustration Style
- Abstract, geometric representations of the research process
- Animated diagrams showing the Brenner Loop
- Agent avatars with distinct visual personalities
- Connection lines showing coordination flows

## Hero Components

### 1. Main Headline
Something like:
- "Research With Rigor. Orchestrate AI Agents Like a Lab."
- "The Scientific Method, Multiplied by AI Agents"
- "From Hunches to Hypotheses to Tests to Truth"

### 2. Subheadline
2-3 sentences explaining the core value:
- Multi-agent research sessions
- Structured artifact production
- Brenner's methodology encoded

### 3. Hero Visualization
Animated diagram showing:
- Multiple agents (Claude, GPT, Gemini icons)
- Arrows showing coordination flow
- Artifacts emerging (hypothesis slates, tests, evidence)
- The loop nature of the process

### 4. CTA Buttons
- Primary: "Get Started" → Installation guide
- Secondary: "Watch Demo" → Video or interactive walkthrough
- Tertiary: "Read the Method" → Brenner method docs

### 5. Social Proof (if available)
- GitHub stars badge
- "Used by researchers at..." (if applicable)
- Test count badge (4300+ tests passing)

## Desktop vs Mobile

### Desktop Hero
- Full-width with generous padding (120px+ vertical)
- Hero visualization on right, text on left
- Floating code snippet showing a session command
- Parallax background elements

### Mobile Hero
- Stacked layout (text above visualization)
- Smaller but still impactful animation
- Sticky CTA at bottom of viewport
- Swipe hint for "scroll to explore"

## Animation Details

### Entry Animations (on load)
1. Background gradient fades in (0-300ms)
2. Headline types in or fades up (300-600ms)
3. Subheadline fades up (600-900ms)
4. Hero visualization assembles (900-1500ms)
5. CTAs fade up (1500-1800ms)

### Scroll Interactions
- Parallax on background elements
- Hero visualization shrinks/fades as user scrolls
- Smooth transition to first content section

## Technical Requirements
- Framer Motion for all animations
- Prefers-reduced-motion support
- No layout shift (reserve space for animated elements)
- Lazy load heavy visualizations
- SVG-based illustrations for crisp scaling","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-05T23:52:02.072882969-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-05T23:52:02.072882969-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.1","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-05T23:52:02.104639745-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.1","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-05T23:58:27.655949672-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.1","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.10","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-05T23:58:29.204979154-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.10","title":"Feature: Mobile-First Responsive Design","description":"# Mobile-First Responsive Design

## Purpose
Ensure the landing page is not just "responsive" but genuinely excellent on mobile. Mobile users should have an experience designed for touch, not a shrunk desktop site.

## Design Philosophy

### Mobile Is Not Desktop Lite
- Different content hierarchy for small screens
- Touch-optimized interactions
- Vertical rhythm and scroll experience
- Battery and bandwidth conscious

### Touch-First Interactions
- 44px minimum touch targets
- Swipe gestures where natural
- No hover-dependent features
- Immediate feedback on touch

### Performance Priority
- Critical CSS inlined
- Images lazy-loaded with proper srcset
- Minimal JavaScript for initial render
- Target: < 2s meaningful paint on 3G

## Breakpoint Strategy

### Breakpoints
- Mobile: 0-639px (default)
- Tablet: 640-1023px (sm:)
- Desktop: 1024-1279px (lg:)
- Wide: 1280px+ (xl:)

### Mobile (< 640px)

#### Hero Section
- Full-width, no side padding hero
- Stacked: headline → visualization → CTAs
- Smaller headline (32px vs 48px)
- Single-column CTA buttons
- Visualization simplified or auto-playing

#### Content Sections
- Single column throughout
- Cards stack vertically
- Horizontal scroll for galleries
- Collapsible for long content

#### Navigation
- Hamburger menu for secondary nav
- Bottom sticky bar for primary CTA
- Breadcrumbs as horizontal scroll

#### Typography
- Body: 16px (never smaller for readability)
- Headlines: 24-32px
- Line height: 1.6 for body text
- Generous paragraph spacing

### Tablet (640-1023px)

#### Hero Section
- Two-column possible (50/50)
- Medium headline size (40px)
- Visualization can be larger

#### Content Sections
- 2-column grids for cards
- Side-by-side comparisons possible
- Tables become horizontally scrollable

### Desktop (1024px+)

#### Full Experience
- All animations enabled
- Hover effects active
- Multi-column layouts
- Large visualizations

## Mobile-Specific Components

### Swipeable Card Carousel
For feature cards, agent profiles, etc.
- Dots indicator below
- Snap scrolling
- Peek next card (shows it continues)
- Touch velocity for momentum

### Expandable Sections
For detailed content:
- Collapsed by default
- Clear expand/collapse affordance
- Smooth height animation
- Remember expanded state

### Bottom Sheet
For detailed views:
- Swipe up to expand
- Swipe down to close
- Handles at top
- Backdrop blur

### Sticky CTA Bar
- Fixed at bottom of viewport
- Primary action always visible
- Appears after scrolling past hero
- Subtle shadow for depth

## Touch Interactions

### Tap
- Immediate visual feedback (scale/opacity)
- 44px minimum target
- Generous spacing between targets

### Swipe
- Natural for carousels
- Gesture hints for new users
- Edge swipe avoided (browser conflict)

### Pull-to-Refresh
- Not needed for static landing page
- But smooth scroll at top

### Long Press
- Not used (confusing pattern)

## Performance Optimizations

### Images
- WebP with PNG fallback
- srcset for density variations
- Lazy loading below fold
- Placeholder blur or skeleton

### Fonts
- System font stack preferred
- If custom: subset, preload, font-display: swap
- Maximum 2 font weights loaded

### JavaScript
- Code split by route
- Dynamic import for below-fold sections
- No blocking scripts
- Service worker for repeat visits

### CSS
- Critical CSS inlined (< 14KB)
- Unused styles tree-shaken
- No render-blocking stylesheets

## Testing Requirements

### Devices to Test
- iPhone SE (small)
- iPhone 14 Pro (medium)
- iPhone 14 Pro Max (large)
- iPad Mini (tablet)
- Various Android sizes

### Conditions to Test
- Portrait and landscape
- With/without notch (safe areas)
- Different font sizes (accessibility)
- Slow 3G connection
- Offline (should show something)

### Metrics Targets
- Lighthouse Mobile: > 90
- First Contentful Paint: < 1.5s
- Largest Contentful Paint: < 2.5s
- Cumulative Layout Shift: < 0.1
- First Input Delay: < 100ms

## Common Mobile Pitfalls to Avoid
- Horizontal scroll on full page
- Tiny touch targets
- Hover-only interactions
- Fixed headers that eat viewport
- Form inputs that trigger zoom
- Slow-loading hero images
- Carousels that hijack scroll","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-05T23:55:52.738313428-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-05T23:55:52.738313428-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.10","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-05T23:55:52.768863403-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.2","title":"Feature: Core Research Workflow Section","description":"# Core Research Workflow Section

## Purpose
This section explains the fundamental workflow: how a research session progresses from question to conclusion. It should make the Session State Machine tangible and exciting.

## Key Messages
1. Research sessions follow a rigorous, reproducible path
2. Every step is tracked, auditable, and reversible
3. The system guides you through Brenner's methodology

## Content Structure

### Section Title
"From Question to Conclusion: The Brenner Loop"

### Phase Visualization
Interactive timeline showing the 11 session phases:
1. **Intake** - Formulate the research question
2. **Sharpening** - Refine hypotheses  
3. **Level-Split** - Separate program from interpreter
4. **Exclusion-Test** - Design discriminative tests
5. **Object-Transpose** - Choose optimal experimental system
6. **Scale-Check** - Validate against physics constraints
7. **Agent-Dispatch** - Convene the tribunal
8. **Synthesis** - Merge agent outputs
9. **Evidence-Gathering** - Collect external evidence
10. **Revision** - Update hypotheses based on evidence
11. **Complete** - Session finished with artifacts

### Visual Treatment
- Horizontal timeline on desktop (scrollable/draggable)
- Vertical stepper on mobile
- Each phase has an icon, title, and 1-line description
- Clicking/tapping expands to show detail
- Current phase highlighted (for demo purposes)

### Feature Callouts

#### Undo/Redo
"Every action is reversible. Explore without fear."
- Show stack visualization
- Mention 50-operation history depth

#### Session Replay
"Reproduce any session exactly. Debug, audit, learn."
- Visual of replay controls (play, pause, step)
- Mention deterministic execution

#### Error Recovery
"Graceful handling when things go wrong."
- Show error state in state machine
- Mention automatic checkpointing

## Animations

### Scroll-Triggered
- Timeline reveals phase by phase as user scrolls
- Each phase icon animates (pulse, glow) when revealed
- Connection lines draw between phases

### Interaction
- Hover on phase = tooltip with more detail
- Click = expand to full description
- Active phase has subtle pulse animation

## Desktop vs Mobile

### Desktop
- Full horizontal timeline
- Phase details appear in panel below
- Side-by-side comparison of "before" and "after" states

### Mobile
- Vertical accordion-style list
- Swipe between phases
- Full-screen detail view on tap
- Progress indicator at top

## Technical Implementation
- Use Framer Motion's useInView for scroll triggers
- Intersection Observer for performance
- Preload phase icons as sprite
- Semantic HTML (timeline should be accessible)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-05T23:52:22.370749691-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-05T23:52:22.370749691-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.2","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-05T23:52:22.401059115-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.2","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-05T23:58:30.474697263-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
-{"id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.3","title":"Feature: Multi-Agent Orchestration Section","description":"# Multi-Agent Orchestration Section

## Purpose
This is the "wow" section - showing how multiple AI agents collaborate like a research team. The visualization should make coordination tangible and the debate formats exciting.

## Key Messages
1. Three specialized agents with distinct roles
2. Structured debates that sharpen arguments
3. Deterministic merging produces auditable artifacts
4. Human stays in control throughout

## Content Structure

### Section Title
"Your Research Team: AI Agents That Debate, Challenge, and Synthesize"

### Agent Profiles
Three cards showing the tribunal:

#### Hypothesis Generator (powered by GPT)
- Icon: Lightbulb or brain
- Role: "Hunt paradoxes, propose hypotheses"
- Personality: Creative, divergent thinking
- Quote: "What if both established models are wrong?"

#### Test Designer (powered by Claude)  
- Icon: Flask or target
- Role: "Design discriminative tests with potency controls"
- Personality: Rigorous, detail-oriented
- Quote: "This test will eliminate half our hypotheses in one observation."

#### Adversarial Critic (powered by Gemini)
- Icon: Shield or sword
- Role: "Attack framing, check scale constraints"
- Personality: Skeptical, thorough
- Quote: "Have you considered that the entire premise might be wrong?"

### Debate Formats
Show the three debate modes:

#### Oxford Style
- Proposition vs Opposition with Judge
- Best for: Testing hypothesis strength
- Visual: Two agents facing off, third observing

#### Socratic
- Probing questions reveal weaknesses
- Best for: Finding hidden assumptions
- Visual: One agent questioning another

#### Steelman Contest
- Each agent builds AND attacks strongest version
- Best for: Exploring hypothesis space
- Visual: Agents building then attacking structures

### Coordination Visualization
Animated diagram showing:
1. Kickoff message sent to all agents
2. Agents respond with structured deltas
3. Deltas merge into unified artifact
4. Human reviews and approves

### Agent Mail Highlight
"Coordination Without Chaos"
- Show message flow diagram
- Mention thread IDs as join keys
- Highlight acknowledgement tracking

## Animations

### Agent Cards
- Cards float/hover slightly on desktop
- Subtle 3D tilt effect on hover
- Agent icon animates (pulses) when "active"

### Debate Visualization
- Animated speech bubbles between agents
- Lines connecting debating agents
- Consensus points highlight in green
- Disagreements highlight in amber

### Message Flow
- Animated dots flowing along connection lines
- Messages appear and merge at artifact node
- Satisfying "completion" animation when merge finishes

## Desktop vs Mobile

### Desktop
- Three agent cards side-by-side
- Large debate format visualization below
- Animated message flow on the side

### Mobile
- Agent cards in swipeable carousel
- Debate formats as expandable cards
- Simplified message flow diagram
- Full-screen debate visualization on tap

## Interactive Elements
- Click agent to see example output
- Toggle between debate formats
- "Start Demo Debate" button (if we build interactive demo)","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-05T23:52:43.398754053-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-06T00:00:26.988455745-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.3","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-05T23:52:43.430354234-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.3","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-05T23:58:31.716874421-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.4","title":"Feature: Quality Assurance & Research Hygiene Section","description":"# Quality Assurance & Research Hygiene Section

## Purpose
Show how the system prevents common research failures: hindsight bias, unfalsifiable hypotheses, ignored confounds, overconfidence. This section builds trust by showing rigor.

## Key Messages
1. The system catches mistakes before they become problems
2. Predictions are locked to prevent hindsight bias
3. Confounds are detected automatically by domain
4. Your confidence accuracy is tracked over time

## Content Structure

### Section Title
"Research Hygiene: Built-In Guardrails for Rigorous Science"

### Feature Blocks

#### Coach Mode
**"Learn the Method While You Work"**
- Progressive scaffolding based on experience level
- Inline explanations with Brenner quotes
- Quality checkpoints catch common mistakes
- Visual: Screenshot of coach explanation appearing

Key points to highlight:
- Three levels: beginner, intermediate, advanced
- Auto-promotes based on tracked progress
- Can be turned off for experts
- Specific feedback, not generic warnings

#### Prediction Lock
**"Prevent Hindsight Bias"**
- Predictions must be locked before test results
- Cannot "predict" outcomes you already know
- Calibration tracked over time

Visual: Timeline showing:
1. Design test
2. Enter predictions
3. LOCK (padlock animation)
4. Run test
5. Compare to locked predictions

#### Calibration Tracking
**"Know Your Accuracy"**
- Brier score for probabilistic accuracy
- Overconfidence bias detection
- Domain-specific tracking
- Visual: Calibration chart (predicted vs actual)

#### Confound Detection
**"See What You Might Miss"**
- 8 research domains with domain-specific confounds
- Automatic detection based on hypothesis text
- Prompting questions for each confound

Domain badges: Psychology, Epidemiology, Economics, Biology, Sociology, Computer Science, Neuroscience, General

Example confounds shown:
- "Selection bias detected - how will you ensure random sampling?"
- "Reverse causation possible - can you establish temporal order?"

#### Artifact Linting
**"50+ Rules for Research Hygiene"**
- Structural integrity checks
- Hypothesis hygiene (third alternative required)
- Test design validation (potency controls required)
- Citation hygiene (anchors must resolve)

Visual: Linter output with green checks and warnings

### Comparison Visual
"Without Guardrails" vs "With Brenner Lab"

| Without | With |
|---------|------|
| Predictions adjusted after seeing results | Predictions locked before execution |
| Confounds discovered in peer review | Confounds flagged during design |
| Vague hypotheses survive | Unfalsifiable language caught |
| Overconfidence unchecked | Calibration tracked and displayed |

## Animations

### Coach Mode Demo
- Animated panel sliding in from side
- Explanation text typing effect
- "Got it!" button with satisfying click animation

### Prediction Lock
- Padlock closing animation (satisfying click)
- Lock icon shakes if user tries to edit
- Timeline filling in as steps complete

### Confound Detection
- Hypothesis text with highlighted keywords
- Confounds appearing as connected tags
- Prompting questions fading in

### Linter
- Code-style output with lines appearing
- Green checkmarks animating in
- Warnings with subtle pulse

## Desktop vs Mobile

### Desktop
- Four feature cards in 2x2 grid
- Hover reveals more detail
- Side-by-side comparison table

### Mobile
- Vertical stack of expandable cards
- Swipe to reveal comparison
- Simplified linter output","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-05T23:53:06.782774811-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-05T23:53:06.782774811-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.4","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-05T23:53:06.813308396-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.4","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-05T23:58:37.948993006-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.5","title":"Feature: Discovery & Intelligence Section","description":"# Discovery & Intelligence Section

## Purpose
Show the "smart" features: finding similar hypotheses, exploring what-if scenarios, and tracking robustness. These features differentiate Brenner Lab from simple chat interfaces.

## Key Messages
1. Connect to your past research automatically
2. Explore evidence impact before running tests
3. Know which hypotheses are robust vs fragile
4. Surface duplicates and clusters

## Content Structure

### Section Title
"Intelligence Built In: Search, Simulate, Score"

### Feature Blocks

#### Hypothesis Similarity Search
**"Find Related Work Instantly"**
- Hash-based embeddings work offline
- No API calls, no usage limits
- Surfaces related hypotheses across sessions
- Identifies potential duplicates

Visual: Search interface showing:
- Query hypothesis
- Similar matches with scores
- Breakdown: statement (0.8), mechanism (0.6), domain (0.9)

Key benefits:
- Don't re-investigate killed hypotheses
- Find forgotten related work
- Identify clusters of research questions

Technical callout: "Works entirely client-side - your hypotheses never leave your machine"

#### What-If Scenarios
**"Simulate Before You Experiment"**
- See how test outcomes would affect confidence
- Compare tests by expected information gain
- Prioritize high-impact experiments

Visual: Scenario builder showing:
- Starting confidence: 60%
- If test supports: → 78%
- If test challenges: → 35%
- Expected information gain: 0.42

"Best next test" recommendation with reasoning

#### Robustness Scoring
**"Know Which Hypotheses Are Battle-Tested"**
- Evidence-weighted survival scores
- Separate scores for supporting vs challenging evidence
- Visual robustness indicator (fragile → robust)

Visual: Hypothesis card with robustness meter:
- H1: "Morphogen gradient" - Robustness: 0.72 (High)
  - 3 supporting tests, 1 challenging (survived)
- H2: "Timing mechanism" - Robustness: 0.35 (Low)
  - 1 supporting test, 2 inconclusive

#### Anomaly Detection
**"Quarantine the Weird, Don't Hide It"**
- Anomalies are tracked, not swept under the rug
- Explicit status: active, resolved, deferred, paradigm-shifting
- Can spawn new hypotheses (paradox grounding)

Visual: Anomaly register with:
- X-001: "Oscillating fate markers" - Status: Active
- "Conflicts with H1, H2 - neither model predicts oscillation"
- "Spawned H4: Bistable genetic circuit hypothesis"

### Interactive Demo Concept
"Try Similarity Search"
- Input field for a sample hypothesis
- Shows mock results with similarity breakdown
- "This would find 3 related hypotheses in your history"

## Animations

### Search Results
- Results fade in one by one (staggered)
- Similarity scores count up
- Match highlights pulse briefly

### What-If
- Confidence bar animating between states
- "If supports" and "If challenges" branches animate
- Recommendation appears with subtle glow

### Robustness
- Meter fills from left to right
- Evidence icons stack up under hypothesis
- Color shifts from red (fragile) to green (robust)

### Anomaly
- Anomaly card has subtle "glitch" animation
- Connection lines to conflicting hypotheses
- "Spawned" arrow animation to new hypothesis

## Desktop vs Mobile

### Desktop
- Feature cards in horizontal scroll or grid
- Interactive demos inline
- Side-by-side before/after visualizations

### Mobile
- Vertical stack with expandable details
- Simplified interactive elements
- Swipe to see different scenarios","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-05T23:53:33.487801561-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-05T23:53:33.487801561-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.5","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-05T23:53:33.520578125-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.5","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-05T23:58:38.966934611-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"brenner_bot-f8vs.6","title":"Feature: Operator Framework Section","description":"# Operator Framework Section

## Purpose
Show the cognitive operators that encode Brenner's methodology. This is intellectually exciting - showing how abstract thinking patterns become composable, executable functions.

## Key Messages
1. Brenner's methods encoded as operators
2. Operators are composable into pipelines
3. Each operator has templates to get started
4. Mathematical notation makes the method precise

## Content Structure

### Section Title
"The Operator Algebra: Brenner's Methods as Executable Code"

### Introduction
"Sydney Brenner's breakthrough wasn't just his discoveries - it was his method. We've encoded his cognitive patterns as composable operators that you can apply systematically."

### Core Operators Display

#### ⊘ Level-Split
**"Separate program from interpreter"**
- Message vs machine, genotype vs phenotype
- Includes "chastity vs impotence" diagnostic
- Template: "What is the information? What is the mechanism?"

Visual: Split diagram showing two levels with examples

#### ✂ Exclusion-Test  
**"Design tests that eliminate, not confirm"**
- Forbidden patterns: what cannot occur if H is true
- Discriminative power rating (1-5)
- Template: "If H1 is true, we should NEVER see..."

Visual: Hypothesis tree with branches being pruned

#### ⟂ Object-Transpose
**"Change the system until the test is easy"**
- Choose organism/model strategically
- "The experimental object is a design variable"
- Template: "What system would make this test cheap and unambiguous?"

Visual: Multiple organisms/systems with selection arrow

#### ⊞ Scale-Check
**"Stay imprisoned in physics"**
- Validate against physical constraints
- Calculate timescales, length scales, energy scales
- Template: "Is this physically possible at the relevant scale?"

Visual: Scale bars with constraint ranges

### The Core Composition
"The signature Brenner move expressed as operator composition:"

```
(⌂ ∘ ✂ ∘ ≡ ∘ ⊘) powered by (↑ ∘ ⟂ ∘ 🔧) constrained by (⊞) kept honest by (ΔE ∘ †)
```

Expanded explanation:
- Start from a paradox (◊)
- Split levels (⊘)
- Extract invariants (≡)
- Design exclusion tests (✂)
- Materialize as decision procedure (⌂)
- Power by amplification (↑) in well-chosen system (⟂) you build yourself (🔧)
- Constrain by physics (⊞)
- Keep honest with exception handling (ΔE) and theory killing (†)

### Extended Operators (collapsed by default)
- ↑ Amplify: Use selection, dominance, regime switches
- ◊ Paradox-hunt: Use contradictions as beacons
- ⊕ Cross-domain: Import tools from other fields
- ∿ Dephase: Work out of phase with fashion
- † Theory-kill: Drop hypotheses when the world says no
- ⌂ Materialize: "What would I see if this were true?"

### Code Example
```typescript
import { pipe } from "@/lib/brenner-loop/operators/framework";

const brennerPipeline = pipe(
  levelSplit,        // Separate levels
  invariantExtract,  // Find what survives
  exclusionTest,     // Design killing experiments
  materialize,       // Compile to decision procedure
);

const result = brennerPipeline(hypothesis, context);
```

## Animations

### Operator Cards
- Each operator symbol has subtle glow/pulse
- Hovering shows expanded description
- Click animates to full explanation

### Composition Formula
- Operators appear one by one as user scrolls
- Connection symbols (∘) draw between them
- Final formula assembles with satisfying animation

### Pipeline Demo
- Code block with syntax highlighting
- Function calls highlight in sequence
- Arrow shows data flowing through pipeline

## Desktop vs Mobile

### Desktop
- Four core operators in 2x2 grid with large symbols
- Composition formula as prominent display
- Code example with live syntax highlighting
- Extended operators in expandable section

### Mobile
- Vertical stack of operator cards
- Swipe to reveal each operator
- Simplified composition (just the formula, expandable explanation)

+// ============================================================================
+// Operator-Aware Quote Matching (bead brenner_bot-v2zy)
+// ============================================================================
+
+/**
+ * A quote ranked by both semantic similarity and operator relevance.
+ */
+export interface RankedQuote extends Quote {
+  /** Semantic similarity score (0-1, from embeddings) */
+  semanticScore: number;
+
+  /** Operator relevance score (0-1, from keyword matching) */
+  operatorRelevance: number;
+
+  /** Combined score for final ranking */
+  combinedScore: number;
+}
+
+/**
+ * Keywords and phrases that indicate relevance to each operator.
+ * Quotes containing these terms score higher when that operator is active.
+ */
+export const OPERATOR_QUOTE_KEYWORDS: Record<OperatorType, string[]> = {
+  level_split: [
+    "level", "levels", "conflat", "program", "interpreter",
+    "mechanism", "implementation", "substrate", "abstraction",
+    "separate", "distinguish", "hierarchy", "layer",
+    "software", "hardware", "algorithm", "computation",
+    "functional", "physical", "description", "explanation",
+    "top-down", "bottom-up", "emergence", "reduction",
+  ],
+  exclusion_test: [
+    "test", "exclude", "exclusion", "discriminat", "falsif",
+    "rule out", "ruling out", "eliminate", "predict",
+    "observation", "experiment", "evidence", "potency",
+    "control", "benchmark", "positive control", "negative control",
+    "diagnostic", "critical experiment", "crucial experiment",
+    "hypothesis", "alternative", "differential diagnosis",
+  ],
+  object_transpose: [
+    "object", "transpose", "system", "model", "organism",
+    "experimental system", "approach", "assay", "method",
+    "zebrafish", "drosophila", "c. elegans", "mouse", "yeast",
+    "in vitro", "in vivo", "cell culture", "organoid",
+    "invariant", "transfer", "translate", "generalize",
+    "conserve", "homolog", "analog", "parallel",
+  ],
+  scale_check: [
+    "scale", "order of magnitude", "dimensional", "unit",
+    "calculation", "estimate", "plausib", "feasib",
+    "energy", "time", "size", "concentration", "rate",
+    "physics", "thermodynamic", "kinetic", "diffusion",
+    "constraint", "limit", "bound", "maximum", "minimum",
+    "back of envelope", "rough calculation", "sanity check",
+  ],
+};
+
+/**
+ * Compute how relevant a quote is to a specific operator context.
+ *
+ * Scores are based on keyword matching - quotes that discuss concepts
+ * relevant to the operator (e.g., "levels" for level_split) rank higher.
+ *
+ * @param quote - The quote to score
+ * @param operator - The current operator context
+ * @returns Score from 0 to 1
+ */
+export function computeOperatorRelevance(quote: Quote, operator: OperatorType): number {
+  const keywords = OPERATOR_QUOTE_KEYWORDS[operator];
+  if (!keywords || keywords.length === 0) return 0;
+
+  // Combine quote text and context for analysis
+  const textToAnalyze = `${quote.quote} ${quote.context || ""}`.toLowerCase();
+
+  // Count keyword matches (partial matching for stems like "conflat" -> "conflation")
+  let matchCount = 0;
+  for (const keyword of keywords) {
+    if (textToAnalyze.includes(keyword.toLowerCase())) {
+      matchCount++;
+    }
+  }
+
+  // Normalize by keyword count, with diminishing returns
+  // 1 match = 0.3, 2 = 0.5, 3 = 0.65, 4+ = 0.75-0.95
+  if (matchCount === 0) return 0;
+  if (matchCount === 1) return 0.3;
+  if (matchCount === 2) return 0.5;
+  if (matchCount === 3) return 0.65;
+
+  // Diminishing returns for 4+, max out at 0.95
+  return Math.min(0.95, 0.65 + (matchCount - 3) * 0.1);
+}
+
+/**
+ * Find quotes relevant to a hypothesis in a specific operator context.
+ *
+ * This combines:
+ * 1. Semantic similarity (from embeddings)
+ * 2. Operator relevance (from keyword matching)
+ *
+ * Quotes are ranked by a weighted combination of both scores.
+ *
+ * @param hypothesis - The hypothesis being analyzed
+ * @param operator - The current operator (level_split, exclusion_test, etc.)
+ * @param entries - The embedding entries to search
+ * @param options - Configuration options
+ * @returns Ranked quotes sorted by combined score
+ */
+export function findRelevantQuotes(
+  hypothesis: HypothesisCard,
+  operator: OperatorType,
+  entries: EmbeddingEntry[],
+  options: {
+    /** How many candidate quotes to consider (default: 20) */
+    candidateLimit?: number;
+    /** How many results to return (default: 5) */
+    resultLimit?: number;
+    /** Weight for semantic score vs operator relevance (default: 0.6) */
+    semanticWeight?: number;
+  } = {}
+): RankedQuote[] {
+  const {
+    candidateLimit = 20,
+    resultLimit = 5,
+    semanticWeight = 0.6,
+  } = options;
+
+  // Build query from hypothesis context
+  const queryText = buildQuoteQueryText([
+    hypothesis.statement,
+    hypothesis.mechanism,
+    ...(hypothesis.predictionsIfTrue || []),
+  ]);
+
+  if (!queryText || entries.length === 0) {
+    return [];
+  }
+
+  // Get semantic candidates (more than we need, to allow reranking)
+  const candidates = findSimilar(queryText, entries, candidateLimit);
+
+  // Convert and score each candidate
+  const rankedQuotes: RankedQuote[] = candidates.map((entry, index) => {
+    const quote = embeddingEntryToQuote(entry);
+
+    // Semantic score: inversely proportional to rank (top result = 1.0)
+    const semanticScore = 1 - (index / candidateLimit);
+
+    // Operator relevance from keyword matching
+    const operatorRelevance = computeOperatorRelevance(quote, operator);
+
+    // Combined score with configurable weighting
+    const operatorWeight = 1 - semanticWeight;
+    const combinedScore = (semanticScore * semanticWeight) + (operatorRelevance * operatorWeight);
+
+    return {
+      ...quote,
+      semanticScore,
+      operatorRelevance,
+      combinedScore,
+    };
+  });
+
+  // Sort by combined score and return top results
+  return rankedQuotes
+    .sort((a, b) => b.combinedScore - a.combinedScore)
+    .slice(0, resultLimit);
+}
+
+/**
+ * Get quotes relevant to a specific operator (without hypothesis context).
+ *
+ * Useful for showing "example quotes" or "Brenner says about level split..."
+ *
+ * @param operator - The operator to find quotes for
+ * @param entries - The embedding entries to search
+ * @param limit - How many quotes to return (default: 5)
+ * @returns Quotes ranked by operator relevance
+ */
+export function getOperatorQuotes(
+  operator: OperatorType,
+  entries: EmbeddingEntry[],
+  limit: number = 5
+): RankedQuote[] {
+  // Score all entries by operator relevance
+  const scored: RankedQuote[] = entries.map((entry) => {
+    const quote = embeddingEntryToQuote(entry);
+    const operatorRelevance = computeOperatorRelevance(quote, operator);
+
+    return {
+      ...quote,
+      semanticScore: 0, // Not using semantic search
+      operatorRelevance,
+      combinedScore: operatorRelevance,
+    };
+  });
+
+  // Return top matches
+  return scored
+    .filter((q) => q.operatorRelevance > 0)
+    .sort((a, b) => b.operatorRelevance - a.operatorRelevance)
+    .slice(0, limit);
+}
+
